original_abstract:
Violence in the healthcare sector has become a growing global public health crisis, characterized by complex biopsychosocial consequences. While violence is a pervasive societal issue, its increasing frequency within healthcare environments is particularly alarming. Recently, the escalation of violence against healthcare professionals in Türkiye and abroad has placed immense physical and psychological strain on doctors and medical staff, leading to a demonstrable decline in job performance and the overall quality of patient care. The World Health Organization has identified this trend as proof of an urgent health emergency that threatens not only the safety of practitioners but the foundational well-being of the entire community.

Despite institutional awareness, healthcare workers across nearly every nation continue to face victimization. In Türkiye, the rapid acceleration of such incidents in recent years has drawn significant public outcry and intensive media coverage. Although cases of violence have been prosecuted under existing provisions of the Turkish Penal Code, these measures have largely proven insufficient as effective deterrents. Consequently, there is an urgent demand for specialized legal frameworks designed specifically to protect healthcare workers. 

This study examines the various types of violence in health and provides a comparative analysis of statistical data from Türkiye and other countries. Furthermore, it details the current and newly enacted legal regulations in Türkiye, outlines the fundamental rights of healthcare workers facing aggression, provides a guide for effective process management following an incident, and offers strategic recommendations for a sustainable solution to this systemic crisis.
humanized_abstract:
Healthcare violence is considered a world-wide emerging public health crisis with multifaceted biopsychosocial impacts. While violence is inevitably a phenomenon observed in many aspects of everyday life, its occurrence in the healthcare setting is far from normal and recently has seen an alarming increase in many countries including our own in Türkiye. The recent increase of violence witnessed against our physicians and healthcare staff in our country and abroad has left them under intense physical and psychological burden, negatively impacting their productivity and the quality of healthcare provided to patients. The recent statement made by WHO on healthcare violence shows us clearly that there is a serious health crisis at hand that not only threatens healthcare professionals, but also has an impact on the health of the whole community.

Despite the fact that most countries of the world are aware of the issue of victimisation of health care workers, it has not yet been eradicated in almost every country in the world. Many cases have been reported in Turkey in recent years and the public and media have reacted strongly. Cases involving violence against health care workers have been prosecuted in accordance with the relevant articles of the new Turkish Penal Code. However, the enforcement of these laws and regulations has not yet been sufficient to serve as an effective deterrent. It is therefore necessary to introduce more specific legislation to protect health care workers.

Violence in Healthcare: Types and Statistical Comparisons with Türkiye and Other Countries In this study, types of violence in healthcare are examined and statistical values from Türkiye and other countries are compared. The study also refers to the applicable legislation in Türkiye as it stands today and with the latest amendments, the rights of healthcare workers who become victims of violence in the workplace, and the steps to take from the beginning to the end of the incident in the best way possible with the provided process management guide, and finally, the recommendations for addressing this ongoing and ever increasing national problem sustainably.
